text: The patient undergoes laparoscopic esophagectomy and gastric pull-up for reconstruction, following recovery from mediastinitis.
The surgeon divides all vascular pedicles except for the gastroepiploic arch during the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses ultrasonic dissection to mobilize the stomach, beginning with the division of the gastrocolic ligament while carefully preserving the gastroepiploic vessels.
The surgeon uses an ultrasonic dissector to divide the short gastric vessels, ensuring precise separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon approaches the left gastric vessels from below the stomach, preparing to isolate and divide these vessels as part of the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon visualizes and dissects the right and left cruciate ligaments, freeing the abdominal portion of the esophagus from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon divides the left gastric vein between absorbable clips, ensuring secure ligation of the vessel.
The surgeon divides the left gastric artery between clips while carefully preserving the accessory hepatic artery, which arises from a common trunk.
The surgeon uses multiple linear stapler applications to fashion the gastric tube, shaping the stomach into a narrow conduit.
The surgeon inserts an instrument through the right-sided cannula, preparing for further surgical manipulation within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon inserts an instrument through the left-sided cannula, capturing the remnant shots of the surgical field.

The surgeon widens the hiatus using the Pinotti technique and performs blunt and ultrasonic dissection of the esophagus up to the level of the esikos vein, following a plane along the anterior surface of the aorta.
The surgeon carefully avoids opening the pleural sheath on both sides during the procedure, maintaining the integrity of the pleural lining.
The surgeon applies upward traction to the mediastinal structures, taking care to avoid any alteration in the cardiac rhythm during the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the upper esophagus from the neck, ensuring the esophagus is fully separated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs the gastric pull-up by applying gentle traction on the esophagus through the neck incision, while monitoring the procedure with a laparoscope.
The surgeon completes the tailoring of the gastric tube by applying two additional linear stapler applications, ensuring the tube is of appropriate length.
The surgeon inserts a soft silicone drainage tube through the hiatus to facilitate drainage of the mediastinum.
The surgeon performs the esophagogastric anastomosis using the Orringer technique, visually connecting the esophagus to the gastric tube.
The surgeon closes the axis opening using interrupted absorbable sutures, securing the area with precise stitching.
The bottom end of the gastric tube is wrapped around the posterior aspect of the proximal esophageal stump, visually securing the gastric tube in place.
